content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 20, 1991


Terry Majuk
Legislative Assistant
California Association of Hospitals
and Health Systems
P. O. Box 1100
Sacramento, CA  95812-1100

Re:  Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-91-408

Dear Ms. Majuk

You have requested advice on behalf of the California
Association of Hospitals and Health Systems (CAHHS) concerning the
application of the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").1

QUESTION

If CAHHS, a lobbyist employer, offers a complimentary
registration fee of $300 to officials or designated employees to
attend its organization's conference, will the officials or
designated employees be receiving a gift or informational
material?

CONCLUSION

Pending a regulation on this issue, the part of the
registration fee that is for admission to the meeting will be
informational material.

FACTS

CAHHS will be holding its annual meeting in October.
Individuals in the health care industry will attend the meeting.
Sessions of the meeting will focus on the impending changes for
health care in the 1990s.  CAHHS is considering waiving the $300
registration fee for various public officials whose duties are
"directly related to health issues."  The officials and employees

___________________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-408
Page 2


will be invited only to attend sessions that provide information
on health care issues and will assist the officials in the
performance of their official duties. The registration fee will
include the cost of some meals.

ANALYSIS

The Act provides that a gift is "any payment to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received . . ."
(Section 82028.) The Act also provides that "the term "gift"
does not include [i]informational material such as books, reports,
pamphlets, calendars, or periodicals." (Section 82028(b)(1).) We
have applied this exception to the definition of gift in the
context of admission to informational conferences. (Newman Advice
Letter, No. A-84-187.)

As structured, it appears that the complimentary admission to
the CAHHS meeting is admission to an informational conference.
Only those officials whose duties are directly related to health
issues will receive the complimentary admission. Those officials
are invited to attend only those sessions of the meeting that
will assist the officials in the performance of their official
duties. Therefore, admission to those events would fall within
the informational conference category of the informational
material exception. This exception only encompasses the free
admission portion of the registration, however. Any portion of
the registration that provides for meals would be a gift.
(Regulation 18228(a).)

Also please be advised that the Commission is currently
considering regulations on this particular issue. Accordingly,
this advice may change as a result of those regulations.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter
please contact me at (916) 322-5901.2

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Mark T. Morodomi
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MM:aa


2 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.analysis:
The Act provides that a gift is "any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received . . ." (Section 82028.) The Act also provides that "The term 'gift' does not include [i]nformational material such as books, reports, pamphlets, calendars, or periodicals." (Section 82028(b)(1).) We have applied this exception to the definition of gift in the context of admission to informational conferences. (Newman Advice Letter, No. A-84-187.)

As structured, it appears that the complimentary admission to the CAHHS meeting is admission to an informational conference. Only those officials whose duties are directly related to health issues will receive the complimentary admission. Those officials are invited to attend only those sessions of the meeting that will assist the officials in the performance of their official duties. Therefore, admission to those events would fall within the informational conference category of the informational material exception. This exception only encompasses the free admission portion of the registration, however. Any portion of the registration that provides for meals would be a gift. (Regulation 18228(a).)

Also please be advised that the Commission is currently considering regulations on this particular issue. Accordingly, this advice may change as a result of those regulations.
